Wrong….. People keep saying the President would need Congress approval to “go to war with Venezuela.” That misunderstands what an arrest versus a war actually is. An arrest pursuant to a federal indictment is a law enforcement action, not a declaration of war. If a foreign national is indicted by the U.S. Department of Justice, the DOJ has authority to pursue that arrest. That authority does not come from Congress approving a war. The military’s role in a situation like that would be protective and logistical, not combat operations against a sovereign state. Providing security does not convert a law enforcement action into an act of war. A declaration of war targets a nation. An arrest targets individuals named in an indictment. Those are legally and constitutionally different actions.

Two things tend to happen in places like Venezuela when a strongman collapses or loses control. First, when a dictator is in charge, he often keeps drugs, gangs, and militias partially contained through brute force deals and fear. It’s ugly, but there’s a kind of centralized control. When that control disappears without a strong, legitimate replacement, power fragments. Second, once power fragments, you get a power vacuum. That’s when gangs, cartels, and armed groups all become powerful at the same time. No one is strong enough to dominate, so you get lawlessness, turf wars, and chaos. That’s how countries slide into anarchy. We’ve seen versions of this in Colombia during its worst years. When the state was weak, figures like Pablo Escobar didn’t just exist. They flourished. Imagine that dynamic multiplied across cities, not centralized under one figure. If a weak leader is installed without real authority or institutional backing, the situation can get worse, not better. Venezuela has long coastlines, major shipping routes, and proximity to global trafficking corridors. In a true power vacuum, that becomes heaven for illicit activity: drugs, weapons, human trafficking, money laundering. So the fear isn’t ideology. It’s stability. Removing a bad system without a strong replacement doesn’t automatically produce democracy. Sometimes it produces chaos. And once chaos sets in, rebuilding order is far harder than people realize.
